title: Git Foundations
subtitle: Build one project history step by step, then prove the same Git habits in new repositories.
description: A beginner local-Git course organized around three cumulative repositories. Guided lessons grow one community-site repository across the whole course, module projects continue one parallel volunteer-hub repository, and the final capstone starts a fresh neighborhood-guide repository. Learners inspect, change, and verify repository state without branches, remotes, GitHub, or assumed web-development knowledge.
audience: Beginners comfortable with basic terminal navigation and simple text-file editing.
Learners who want local Git foundations before branches, remotes, or GitHub.
Learners who do not need prior HTML, CSS, or JavaScript knowledge because every file edit is supplied exactly.
prerequisites: Complete Linux Terminal Fundamentals or be comfortable using pwd, ls, cd, mkdir, touch, cat, cp, mv, and rm in a terminal.
Know how to open, edit, and save a simple text file in the provided workspace.
No HTML, CSS, JavaScript, GitHub account, or previous version-control experience is required. Every required file edit supplies the exact text and location.
goals: Explain the working tree, staging area, commits, and HEAD using one project that grows across the course.
Initialize a repository, configure a repository-only identity, and read tracked, untracked, staged, modified, ignored, and clean state.
Inspect exact file changes, stage one purpose at a time, create meaningful commits, and read the resulting history.
Rename, remove, ignore, unstage, and restore files safely without rewriting history.
Reapply the same essential workflow in one cumulative module project and one fresh final capstone.

teachingStyle.tone: Practical, book-like, reassuring, and precise. Continue one project story instead of resetting context. Define each Git idea once, then reuse it.

preferredTerms.run button: type the command and press Enter
preferredTerms.repository state: the working tree, staging area, and commit history Git currently records

notes: Use one cumulative guided repository named community-site for every technical teaching exercise in Modules 1-3.

Use one cumulative parallel module-project repository named volunteer-hub across all three module projects; Module 2 begins from Module 1’s canonical final state, and Module 3 begins from Module 2’s canonical final state.
Use one fresh cumulative capstone repository named neighborhood-guide for all six capstone steps.
Every exercise must be independently loadable: reconstruct the exact canonical prerequisite repository state in hidden setup rather than depending on a previous browser session.

For each cumulative stream, the canonical solution state of milestone N must equal the canonical starter state of milestone N+1, including files, commit history, staged state, unstaged state, ignored state, and terminal working directory.
This is a Git course, not a web-development course. Never assume HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Markdown, or shell-scripting knowledge beyond the stated prerequisites.

Whenever learners must edit a file, state the exact file path, the exact text to add or replace, and the exact insertion or replacement location. Grade the repository transition, not web syntax or design skill.
Keep file contents short, readable, and meaningful as Git changes. Do not ask learners to invent page structure, styling, scripts, copy, or filenames.
Teach each Git concept once in the guided stream. Later guided exercises, module projects, and the capstone may reuse it in a new repository state but must not reteach the same definition.
Keep setup state and grading expectations separate. Hidden setup creates the prerequisite milestone; gitExpectations and focused checks verify only the learner transition.
Use profileId git, fixedLanguage bash, recipeType shell_task, and mode terminal_workspace for every Git code_input.
Use the existing terminal-workspace runner and shared editor/file explorer. Do not create a Git-specific runner or expose hidden setup files.
Configure repository-local identity as ZoeSkoul Learner and learner@zoeskoul.local before the first commit.
Set the initial branch to main whenever initialization is part of the task, without turning main into a branching lesson.
Never grade exact hashes, timestamps, absolute workspace paths, or decorative log formatting.
Use gitExpectations for repository state and terminalExpectations only when the exact command is itself the learning goal.
Every terminal instruction must say to type the command and press Enter.
Keep the course local. Do not introduce branches, merges, remotes, GitHub, stash, reflog, revert, reset, rebase, tags, or force operations.
Use one synopsis sketch for each module project and capstone, followed by exactly the authored number of cumulative steps.
Write lessons like a practical book chapter: continue the project story, define one new Git idea, trace the state change, warn about one common mistake, and transition into practice.
Prepend one reading-only course introduction explaining prerequisites, the three repository journeys, the inspect-change-verify habit, and how deterministic resets preserve cumulative learning.
Keep quiz distractors inside the Git concepts already taught. Do not use branches, remotes, GitHub, merges, or other later-course features as easy wrong answers.
Avoid repeating the same concept check in later topics. Each quiz should assess the new repository layer or decision introduced by its teaching topic.
